verses.commentary: On the third day. John counts days deliberately. A wedding at Cana in Galilee. Jesus enters the ordinary joy of human celebration. His mother is there, showing Jesus' participation in family and community life.
Jesus and his disciples were invited. The new community joins the celebration. Discipleship does not mean withdrawal from life but transformation within it.
The wine ran out. Social disaster. Mary brings the need to Jesus. She expects Him to act, though His public ministry has not yet begun.
Woman, what does this have to do with me? Not harsh but establishing boundaries. My hour has not yet come. Jesus operates on the Father's timeline, not human urgency. Yet He will act.
Do whatever He tells you. Mary's final recorded words in John. Perfect instruction for all disciples. Faith obeys without knowing the plan.
Six stone water jars for Jewish purification rites. Each holding twenty to thirty gallons. The old ceremonial order stands ready for transformation.
Fill the jars with water. Simple command. They filled them to the brim. Complete obedience. Faith acts on Jesus' word before seeing results.
Now draw some out and take it to the master of the banquet. The moment of risk. They obey without knowing what will happen. Faith trusts in the unseen.
Water became wine. Creation ex nihilo. The master tasted without knowing the source. Jesus works invisibly. Only the servants who obeyed knew what happened.
You have saved the best until now. Human pattern reversed. Grace improves on nature. Jesus does not merely restore but elevates. New covenant wine surpasses the old.
The first of the signs. Semeion (σημεῖον), not just miracle but revelation. He revealed His glory. Signs point beyond themselves to Jesus' identity. His disciples believed in Him.
After this He went to Capernaum with His mother, brothers, and disciples. Family and followers together. They stayed a few days. Brief transition before the confrontation in Jerusalem.
The Passover was near. Jesus goes to Jerusalem for the feast. He honors Jewish worship while preparing to fulfill and transform it.
In the temple courts: cattle, sheep, doves, money changers. Necessary for sacrifice but corrupted into commerce. The temple has become a marketplace.
He made a whip of cords. Premeditated action, not spontaneous rage. Drove them all out. Zeal for God's house burns in Jesus. Prophetic sign action.
Stop making My Father's house a marketplace. Possessive language: My Father's house. Jesus claims unique relationship with God and authority over the temple.
His disciples remembered Psalm 69:9. Zeal for your house will consume me. They interpret Jesus' action through Scripture. The cross looms in this psalm of suffering.
What sign do You show us? They demand validation. Authority must be proven. Jesus has challenged their system; they require His credentials.
Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it. Cryptic prophecy. Jesus speaks of His body; they hear building. The true temple is His person.
Forty-six years to build this temple. They mock His claim. Physical impossibility blinds them to spiritual reality. The absurdity hides the deeper truth.
But He spoke of the temple of His body. John's editorial clarification. Jesus Himself is the meeting place between God and humanity. Incarnation replaces institution.
After He was raised from the dead, His disciples remembered. Resurrection unlocks understanding. Believing the Scripture and Jesus' words become unified. Faith matures through fulfillment.
Many believed in His name when they saw the signs. Sign-based faith is real but incomplete. Belief rooted in spectacle is fragile.
But Jesus would not entrust Himself to them. He knew all people. Jesus discerns hearts. Surface belief does not guarantee genuine discipleship. He sees what others cannot.
He did not need anyone to testify about mankind. He knew what was in each person. Divine omniscience. Jesus needs no reports about human nature. He created it and knows its corruption and potential.
